requiredExam.label: Test proximal hip flexor strength

source.source_section: Osteoporosis curated endocrine workup: required bedside maneuver
source.implementation_notes: Guideline-backed endocrine workup content curated into the local knowledge schema; schema, source, PHI, and regression tests run on 2026-06-06.

requiredExam.action: Use this maneuver to evaluate hypocalcemia, osteomalacia/osteoporosis complication, fracture risk, or fall risk. Weakness changes fall risk, severity assessment, and urgency of calcium or vitamin D evaluation.
requiredExam.rationale: Targets hypocalcemia, osteomalacia/osteoporosis complication, fracture risk, or fall risk; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Osteoporosis.
requiredExam.technique: Test hip flexion strength against resistance bilaterally while the patient is seated or supine, adapting to pain and safety.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: When osteomalacia, hypercalcemia, hypocalcemia, falls, or weakness is possible
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: hypocalcemia, osteomalacia/osteoporosis complication, fracture risk, or fall risk
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.
requiredExam.management_change: Weakness changes fall risk, severity assessment, and urgency of calcium or vitamin D evaluation.
requiredExam.limitations: Test proximal hip flexor strength is supportive for Osteoporosis, not diagnostic by itself; reliability can be reduced by pain, positioning, acute illness, cooperation, body habitus, baseline disability, and examiner technique.

source.source_section: Osteoporosis curated endocrine workup: basic bedside data and safety checks
source.implementation_notes: Guideline-backed endocrine workup content curated into the local knowledge schema; schema, source, PHI, and regression tests run on 2026-06-06.

requiredExam.action: Check and document as baseline bedside data for this workup. Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Osteoporosis is the selected validated workup and the maneuver is clinically safe and relevant at bedside.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context
requiredExam.management_change: Hypotension or severe hypertension changes acuity, medication safety, fluid strategy, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Single readings can be affected by cuff size, positioning, pain, anxiety, arrhythmia, recent activity, and medications; repeat or compare with baseline when results change management.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to routine bedside safety data; use this item to assess acuity, monitoring needs, and management safety rather than diagnostic probability.
requiredExam.technique: Measure blood pressure using an appropriately sized cuff after a brief rest period, seated or supine as clinically appropriate.
requiredExam.rationale: Targets hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context; positive, negative, or unable-to-assess findings guide escalation, diagnostic testing, treatment safety, or specialty planning for Osteoporosis.

reference_values: Corrected calcium often about 8.5-10.5 mg/dL; ionized calcium about 1.12-1.32 mmol/L; use local lab.
Intact PTH often about 10-65 pg/mL but assay-specific.
DXA T-score: normal >= -1.0; osteopenia -1.0 to -2.5; osteoporosis <= -2.5.
25(OH)D thresholds vary; many labs flag <20 ng/mL as low and <12 ng/mL as severe deficiency, while 2024 Endocrine Society guidance avoids universal screening/target thresholds for healthy adults.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
decision_steps: Diagnose osteoporosis by fragility fracture, T-score <= -2.5, or high fracture risk; assess vertebral imaging and secondary causes before therapy selection.
Classify fracture risk as high versus very high using recent fracture, multiple fractures, very low BMD, falls, glucocorticoids, and FRAX or local tool.
Check calcium, 25(OH)D, kidney function, PTH or other secondary-cause labs as indicated before antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.
Reassess BMD and fracture risk after therapy interval, especially after 3-5 years of bisphosphonate exposure.
Confirm that Osteoporosis is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.

Order the first-line tests in sequence: DXA and vertebral imaging when indicated; Calcium, phosphorus, creatinine/eGFR, alkaline phosphatase, 25(OH)D, PTH, TSH/CBC/CMP and secondary-cause labs as indicated.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Corrected calcium often about 8.5-10.5 mg/dL; ionized calcium about 1.12-1.32 mmol/L; use local lab. Intact PTH often about 10-65 pg/mL but assay-specific.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Use bisphosphonate first-line for many high-risk patients when renal function, esophageal risk, and adherence permit.
Use anabolic therapy such as teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab for very-high-risk patients, then follow with antiresorptive therapy.
Use denosumab when appropriate, but plan transition therapy if stopping to avoid rebound vertebral fracture risk.
Ensure calcium/vitamin D adequacy, fall prevention, exercise, smoking/alcohol counseling, dental risk review, and secondary-cause treatment.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Osteoporosis; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Osteoporosis, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.
